questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Graphite is a form of carbon and has a layer structure. Graphene is an individual single layer of graphite. Their structures are shown below:

[IMG:q1_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Thin sheets of graphene can be easily peeled off from graphite using adhesive tape.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain why graphene can be easily peeled off.
children.answer: Layers of graphite are held together by van der Waals' forces / weak intermolecular forces only.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain whether graphene can conduct electricity.
children.answer:
Yes, graphene has delocalised electrons / electrons in graphene are not localised / mobile electrons / electrons will flow.
Not accepted: No, .....
electrons / sea of electrons / free electrons

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Draw the electron diagram for a molecule of the compound formed by complete combustion of graphene, showing electrons in the outermost shells only.
children.answer:
[IMG:q1_img_1.jpg]
(Accept any symbols of electrons, ignore shape)
Not accepted: Showing electrons in the inner shells

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Based on the fact that graphene can be easily peeled off from graphite, a student concluded that graphite should have a low melting point due to its layer structure. Explain whether you agree with this conclusion.
sub_questions.answer:
No. Graphene layers are made up of a giant covalent structure.
A large amount of energy is needed during melting to destroy the large amount of strong covalent bonds between atoms.
Not accepted: Yes...

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Fullerene (such as  \( C_{60} \) ) is another form of carbon. Briefly describe the structure of  \( C_{60} \) , and suggest why it is soluble in some organic solvents.
sub_questions.answer:
\( C_{60} \) has a spherical shape (ball) / and with strong covalent bonds between atoms.
\( C_{60} \) has a simple molecular structure.
The van der Waals' forces / attractive forces between \( C_{60} \) molecules are of comparable / similar strength as those in organic solvent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Draw the structure of ethane-1,2-diol, and suggest whether it is soluble in water.
questions.answer:
[IMG:q2_img_0.jpg]

It has a small molecular size. / It is a small molecule. / It has a short carbon chain.
The hydroxyl groups in it can form hydrogen bonds with water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Both polyethene (PE) and 'Saran' can be used to make food wrap, but 'Saran' is more suitable than PE in making food wrap for use in microwave ovens.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: The monomer of PE is ethene. Suggest a chemical test to show that ethene is an unsaturated compound.
sub_questions.answer:
Add in  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  or  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{organic~solvent}) \)  / acidified  \( \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  / neutral or alkaline  \( \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \) . 1
Reddish brown or brown or orange  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  decolourised or becomes colourless (paler) 1
/ Purple  \( \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  decolourised or becomes colourless (paler)
/ Purple  \( \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}(\mathrm{aq}) \)  becomes brown
Not accepted: yellow  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \) ,  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2} \) , Bromine,  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{g}) \) ,  \( \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{l}) \) , ...

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
'Saran' can be formed from the polymerisation of the compound shown below:

[IMG:q3_img_0.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the systematic name of this compound.
children.answer: 1,1-dichloroethene

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Name the type of polymerisation involved in forming 'Saran'.
children.answer:
addition (polymerisation)
Not accepted: additional polymerisation

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Draw the structure of 'Saran', showing at least THREE repeating units.
children.answer:
[IMG:q3_img_1.jpg]
(Need to show 3 or more repeating units, in the following orders: B—B—B etc.)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: In terms of intermolecular force, explain why 'Saran' is more suitable than PE in making food wrap for use in microwave ovens.
sub_questions.answer: ‘Saran’ is more heat resistant / has a higher melting temperature / is less soluble in oil because the polar attraction (force) between ‘Saran’ polymer chains is stronger than that between PE / the molecular sizes of ‘Saran’ are larger, hence it has a larger dispersion force or van del Waal’s forces or intermolecular forces than that in PE.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: When incinerated, why would food wrap made from ‘Saran’ cause more serious pollution problem than food wrap made from PE?
sub_questions.answer: Incineration of food wrap made from ‘Saran’ will produce toxic gases / harmful gases / dioxins / hydrogen chloride / HCl / chlorine /  \( Cl_{2} \) , while that made from PE will not.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: With reference to the methods of obtaining copper, magnesium and silver from their oxides, deduce the order of reactivity of these three metals.
questions.answer:
By heating oxide of silver directly, silver can be obtained, while copper and magnesium cannot be obtained by similar method.
By heating with charcoal / carbon / hydrogen / carbon monoxide / town gas, oxide of copper can be reduced to copper, while magnesium cannot be obtained by similar method.
Magnesium can only be obtained by electrolysis of its oxide in molten state.
As more stable is the metal oxide, the more reactive is the metal. So, the order of reactivity is:  
magnesium > copper > silver
Communication mark (demonstrate the ability to deduce the answer)
(chemical knowledge = 0 to 2, communication mark = 0
 chemical knowledge = 3 to 4, communication mark = 0 or 1 
incomplete answer / difficult to understand, communication mark = 0)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text: Concentrated acids are common reagents found in laboratories.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State a safety measure in handling concentrated acids in laboratories.
sub_questions.answer: Wearing protective gloves or plastic gloves or gown or safety goggles or any suitable PPE / adding concentrated acids into water when diluting the concentrated acids / use a fume cupboard. Not accepted: maintain a good ventilation.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Comment on the following statement:

'All concentrated acids are strong acids.'
sub_questions.answer: No, the strength of an acid is not related to its concentration. / Not all concentrated acids, e.g. ethanoic acid, are strong acids / use a concrete example to illustrate. Not accepted: Yes, .....

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain how concentrated sulphuric acid, concentrated nitric acid and concentrated ethanoic acid can be distinguished by using copper granules.
sub_questions.answer:
Concentrated sulphuric acid reacts with copper to liberate a colourless gas / irritating gas / gas with characteristic smell / block solid (copper(II) oxide).
Concentrated nitric acid reacts with copper to liberate a brown gas / bluish-green or blue solution.
When concentrated ethanoic acid is added to copper granules, no observable changes occur / no reaction.
Not accepted: exothermic / bluish-green or blue solution in concentrated sulphuric acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text: Petrol is a commonly used motor car fuel. It can be obtained from petroleum by fractional distillation.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Explain, from molecular level, why petrol can be obtained from petroleum by fractional distillation.
children.answer:
Components having different boiling points can be separated from each other by fractional distillation.
The longer the carbon chain, the higher is the boiling point.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Other than directly obtaining petrol from fractional distillation of petroleum, suggest a way for producing extra petrol.
children.answer: Cracking of heavy oil/heavy hydrocarbons

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Octane ( \( C_{8}H_{18} \) ) is a component of petrol. Using octane as an example, state the meaning of the term ‘standard enthalpy change of combustion’ with the aid of a chemical equation.
children.answer:
The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound (/ substance / octane) burns completely under standard conditions/25°C and 1 atm.
\(C_{8}H_{18}(l)+\frac{25}{2}O_{2}(g)\rightarrow8CO_{2}(g)+9H_{2}O(l) \) (The eq. should have correct state symbols)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Motor cars powered by petrol emit air pollutants such as nitrogen monoxide and carbon monoxide. Installing a certain device in motor cars can convert these two oxides to less harmful substances.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Name this device.
children.answer: Catalytic converter (†)

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
The equation for the reaction involved in the conversion is shown below: \( 2CO(g)+2NO(g)\rightarrow2CO_{2}(g)+N_{2}(g) \) The standard enthalpy changes of formation of NO(g), CO(g) and CO₂(g) are as follows:
Compound\( \Delta H_{f}^{\circ} \) / kJ mol \( ^{-1} \)
NO(g)+90.3
CO(g)-110.5
CO \( _{2} \) (g)-394.0
Calculate the standard enthalpy change of the above reaction.
children.answer:
\( \Delta H^{\theta}=2(-394)-2(-110.5)-2(90.3) \) 
(1 mark for correct coefficients, 1 mark for correct signs of the terms)
\( =-747.6\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)  (the answer should have correct sign and unit)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
A bottle of concentrated hydrochloric acid HCl(aq) is shown below:

[IMG:q7_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: According to the information on the label, calculate the concentration of the concentrated hydrochloric acid in mol dm\( ^{-3} \) .
sub_questions.answer:
Mass of HCl present in 1000 cm³ of the concentrated acid = 1180 x 36% = 425 g 
Formula mass of HCl = 36.5
Concentration = 425 / 36.5 = 11.6 mol dm⁻³ (M) (Accept 11.5 – 11.644, 12, not accept 12.0) 
(Accept answer without unit) (NOT accept wrong unit)(accept maximum 3 decimal places)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: To find out the concentration of the concentrated acid, a laboratory technician first drew from the bottle a sample of  \( 10.00 \, cm^{3} \)  of the concentrated acid and diluted it to  \( 100.0 \, cm^{3} \)  in a volumetric flask. The diluted acid sample was then used to titrate a standard sodium carbonate solution placed in a conical flask using methyl orange as an indicator.  \( 10.00 \, cm^{3} \)  of 1.06 mol dm \( ^{-3} \)  sodium carbonate solution required  \( 20.30 \, cm^{3} \)  of the diluted acid sample to reach the end point.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Briefly describe the procedure in preparing a standard sodium carbonate solution.
children.answer:
Weigh accurately the amount of sodium carbonate needed and dissolve it using deionised water / distilled water. (accept using “a known amount of sodium carbonate”; not accept if state “water” only)
Transfer all the solution made to a volumetric flask, add deionised water to the graduation mark of the flask, and mix the content thoroughly.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Using the titration result, calculate the concentration, in mol dm\( ^{-3} \) , of the concentrated hydrochloric acid in the bottle.
children.answer:
No. of mole of  \( H^{+} \)  present in the diluted acid =  \( \frac{1.06 \times (10/1000) \times 2}{0.0212} \)  = 0.0212
Concentration of the acid in the bottle =  \( \frac{0.0212}{(20.30/1000) \times 10} \)  = 10.4 mol dm \( ^{-3} \)  (M) 1
(Accept answer without unit)(NOT accept wrong unit)
(accept maximum 3 decimal places)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest a possible reason why the concentration of the concentrated hydrochloric acid in the bottle obtained from (b)(ii) would be smaller than that obtained from (a) above.
sub_questions.answer: Some HCl escaped / vaporised from the concentrated acid as HCl(g) / (Concentrated hydrochloric acid is volatile.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows a set-up in which electrons are flowing through the electric wires. Moreover, one of the electrodes in beaker A is forming ions.

[IMG:q8_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State an expected observation at each of the following electrodes:

children.label: i
children.question_text: electrode W
children.answer: The electrode dissolves / becomes smaller / becomes thinner gradually.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: electrode X
children.answer: (Colourless) bubbles / gas are given out.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Write the half equation for the expected change at each of the following electrodes:

children.label: i
children.question_text: electrode Y

children.label: ii
children.question_text: electrode Z
children.answer: \( Ag^{+} + e^{-} \rightarrow Ag \)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Complete the following table by filling in ‘anode’ or ‘cathode’ to describe the electrodes.
electrode Welectrode Z
anode / cathode

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Predict, with reason, what would happen if the  \( MgSO_{4}(aq) \)  in beaker A is replaced by ethanol.
sub_questions.answer:
Electrons would not flow through the electric wires / no observable changes on all electrodes / no reaction occurs because ethanol is not an electrolyte / cannot conduct electricity.
†: correct spelling

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Consider each of the experiments below and answer the questions that follow.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Dilute sodium hydroxide solution is added to copper(II) sulphate solution.

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the expected observation.
children.answer: A blue precipitate is obtained.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the reaction that occurs.
children.answer:
\( \mathrm{Cu^{2+}(aq) + 2OH^{-}(aq) \rightarrow Cu(OH)_{2}(s) / CuSO_{4} + 2NaOH \rightarrow Cu(OH)_{2} + Na_{2}SO_{4}} \)  
(State symbols are not required)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Acidified potassium permanganate solution is added to sodium sulphite solution.

children.label: i
children.question_text: State the expected colour change.
children.answer: Purple acidified potassium permanganate solution is decolourised / turns into colourless / turns into pale pink.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: For the reaction leading to the colour change,
state the name of the type of reaction; and
children.answer: Redox / reduction (of acidified potassium permanganate) / oxidation-reduction (†)

children.question_text: write the ionic equation for the reaction.
children.answer:
\( 2MnO_{4}^{-}(aq) + 5SO_{3}^{2-}(aq) + 6H^{+}(aq) \rightarrow 2Mn^{2+}(aq) + 5SO_{4}^{2-}(aq) + 3H_{2}O(l) \)  
(State symbols are not required)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
You are provided with common laboratory apparatus, calcium carbonate and 1M hydrochloric acid. Outline how you would perform a fair comparison in studying the effect of different concentrations of acid on the rate of production of carbon dioxide from the following reaction:

\(  CaCO_{3}(s)+2HCl(aq)\rightarrow CaCl_{2}(aq)+H_{2}O(l)+CO_{2}(g) \)
questions.answer:
Proper way to follow the progress of the reaction (e.g. measure the volume of  \( CO_{2} \)  evolved / measure the loss in mass of the reaction mixture over a certain time interval / measure the pressure of the  \( CO_{2} \)  formed in a sealed reaction vessel.) (accept graphical representation) (no communication mark if no description about “time”)
Dilute 1M HCl to different concentrations by adding water.
Repeat the experiment with the diluted HCl
State one requirement for carrying out fair comparison (e.g CaCO \( _{3} \)  used should be of the same amount / under same experimental conditions such as same temperature or pressure)
Communication mark
(chemical knowledge = 0 to 2, communication mark = 0
chemical knowledge = 3 to 4, communication mark = 0 or 1
incomplete answer / difficult to understand, communication mark = 0)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World
questions.question_text:
Vanadium is a transition metal, its chemical symbol is V. The formulae and the colours of three aqueous vanadium-containing ions are shown below:
Formula\(VO^{2+}(aq)\)\(V^{3+}(aq)\)\(V^{2+}(aq)\)
Colourbluegreenviolet

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Based on the given information, suggest TWO properties of vanadium to characterise it as a transition metal.
sub_questions.answer: Vanadium exhibits variable oxidation numbers and its ions in aqueous solutions carry colours.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Vanadium also forms the ion  \( VO_{2}^{+}(aq) \) . In the presence of acid, 1.0 mol of  \( VO_{2}^{+}(aq) \)  ions and 1.0 mol of  \( SO_{2}(g) \)  react completely to form  \( SO_{4}^{2-}(aq) \)  ions and one of the above aqueous vanadium-containing ions.

children.label: i
children.question_text: By considering the amount of electrons transferred, deduce the final colour of the solution obtained.
children.answer:
1 (mol of)  \( VO_{2}^{+}(aq) \)  ions gains 2 (mol of) electrons from 1 (mol of)  \( SO_{4}^{-}(g) \)  to become 1 (mol of)  \( V^{3+} \)  (aq).
\( V^{3+} \)  (aq) is green in colour.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the reaction in (i).
children.answer: \( SO_{2}(g) + VO_{2}^{+}(aq) \rightarrow SO_{4}^{2-}(aq) + V^{3+}(aq) \)  (State symbols are not required)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Benzamide, benzoic acid and benzyl bromide are commonly used organic compounds. Their structures are shown below:

[IMG:q12_img_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text:
In an experiment, benzoic acid is prepared from benzamide in two steps:

Step 1: Benzamide is added to excess 1M NaOH(aq) and the mixture is heated gently. An organic compound X is formed.
Step 2: The resulting mixture is then treated with reagent Y until no more solid benzoic acid is given out.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Name the type of reaction involved in Step 1.
children.answer: (alkaline) hydrolysis ( \( \dagger \) )

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Draw the structure of X.
children.answer: [IMG:q12_img_1.jpg]

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Suggest what Y would be.
children.answer: HCl(aq) / H₂SO₄(aq) (accept other reasonable strong acids; not accept H⁺)

children.label: iv
children.question_text: Suggest why X is more soluble than benzoic acid in water.
children.answer:
X (sodium benzoate) is an ionic compound which has strong(er) interactions with water. / Benzoic acid exists as molecules which has weak(er) intermolecular interactions with water. /
X is an ionic compound while benzoic acid exists as molecules.

children.label: v
children.question_text: Describe briefly how a dry benzoic acid sample can be obtained after Step 2.
children.answer: Filter the mixture to obtain the solid benzoic acid. Wash it with deionised water and then dry in oven. (not accept mixing with drying agents) (not accept evaporation or crystallisation before filtration)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Outline a synthetic route, with no more than three steps, to accomplish the conversion of benzoic acid to benzyl bromide. For each step, give the reagent(s), reaction conditions (as appropriate) and structure of the organic product.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:q12_img_2.jpg]
(not accept using  \( LiAlH_{4} \)  in acidic medium; not accept using  \( NaBH_{4} \)  and catalytic hydrogenation)
[IMG:q12_img_3.jpg]
Correct reagent for each step in the conversion 
Intermediate  \( \left(\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}\right) \)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Consider the reaction represented by the equation below:
\( 2NO(g)+O_{2}(g)\rightleftharpoons2NO_{2}(g) \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: In an experiment, 1.02 mol of NO(g) and 1.29 mol of O₂(g) are mixed in a 50.0 dm³ closed container maintained at 980 K. When equilibrium is attained, 61.0% of NO(g) is consumed.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Calculate the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the above reaction under the experimental conditions.

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Discuss whether  \( K_{c} \)  would change if additional NO(g) is introduced into the above equilibrium mixture.
children.answer: No change, because  \( K_{c} \)  is independent of concentration / only depends on temperature.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
The values of \( K_{c} \) (in appropriate unit) for this reaction at different temperatures are shown below:
Temperature / K600700800900
\( K_{c} \)6.88×1062.97×1052.89×1034.68×102
Based on the above data, deduce whether the forward reaction is exothermic or endothermic.
sub_questions.answer:
As revealed from the data, when temperature increases,  \( K_{c} \)  decreases. Therefore the forward reaction is exothermic. / As higher temperature favours endothermic side of reaction, so the forward reaction is exothermic. / 
As higher temperature favours endothermic side of reaction, so the forward reaction is exothermic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Butter contains a small amount of the triglyceride of butanoic acid.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the structure of the triglyceride of butanoic acid.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: An organic acid Q is an isomer of butanoic acid. State the systematic name of Q.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
The structure of Z, another isomer of butanoic acid, is shown below:

[IMG:q14_img_1.jpg]

children.label: i
children.question_text: Using ‘*’, label ALL chiral centre(s) in the above structure of Z.
children.answer: [IMG:q14_img_2.jpg]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest a chemical test to show how to distinguish between Q and Z.
children.answer:
Correct chemical reagent 
Correct observations with comparison between the tests on Q and Z 

Possible tests and the corresponding observations:
\( Cr_{2}O_{7}^{2-}/H^{+} \)  Observations: Q – no change; Z – from orange to green
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/H^{+} \)  Observations: Q – no change; Z – from purple to colourless
\( MnO_{4}^{-}/OH^{-} \)  Observations: Q – no change; Z – formation of brown ppt.
2,4-DNP Observations: Q – no change; Z – formation of orange ppt.
\( CH_{3}CH_{2}OH/H^{+}/heat \)  Observations: Q–fruity smell compound formed; Z – no change.
\( CH_{3}COOH/H^{+}/heat \)  Observations: Q–no change; Z–fruity smell compound formed.
\( CO_{3}^{2-} \)  Observations: Q – formation of gas ( \( CO_{2} \) ); Z – no change
\( HCO_{3}^{-} \)  Observations: Q – formation of gas ( \( CO_{2} \) ); Z - no change
(also accept other reasonable chemical tests with correct observations stated. E.g. use of suitable acid-base indicators (litmus) or suitable metal (Mg))
(not accept using physical methods or using inappropriate metals (K, Na))
2,4-DNP = 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Margarine, a butter substitute, can be made from vegetable oils. What chemical reaction is involved in the production of margarine from vegetable oils?
